Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness in the age of cognitive science · 20th – 21st c.

Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Analytic philosophy of mind emerges from logic, language, neutral monism, and anti-psychologism. The result is not one doctrine but a family of efforts to clarify thought, consciousness, meaning, and experience with unprecedented conceptual precision.

1842 – 1910

William James

The stream of thought, the self, and a psychology that begins from the inside.

1900 – 1976

Gilbert Ryle

The ghost in the machine dismissed: mind as a set of dispositions and abilities.

1912 – 1954

Alan Turing

A developmental, computational functionalism: could machines think, and how would we know?

mid-20th c.

U. T. Place & J. J. C. Smart

Consciousness is a brain process: the rise of mind-brain identity theory.

1917 – 2003

Donald Davidson

Anomalous monism: mental events are physical events, but mental descriptions are not law-governed.

1926 – 2016

Hilary Putnam

From functionalism to its recantation: what pain is, and why the mind is not a computer in the way we thought.

1935 – 2017

Jerry Fodor

The language of thought and the modularity of mind.

1941 – 2001

David Lewis

Analytic functionalism in a systematic metaphysics: folk psychology, qualia, and modal realism.

b. 1937

Thomas Nagel

What it is like to be a subject: the irreducibility of point of view.

1942 – 2024

Daniel Dennett

The intentional stance, the narrative self, and consciousness as fame in the brain.

b. 1932

John Searle

Biological naturalism, intentionality, and the Chinese Room.

b. 1942

Ned Block

Access consciousness and phenomenal consciousness: a distinction that reframed the debate.

b. 1939

David Rosenthal

Higher-order theories of consciousness: what makes a mental state conscious.

b. 1966

David Chalmers

The hard problem, naturalistic dualism, and the structure of conscious experience.

1872 – 1970

Bertrand Russell

Logical analysis, neutral monism, and the structural relation between mind and world.

1861 – 1947

Alfred North Whitehead

Process, feeling, and a panexperiential metaphysics in which mentality runs deep.

1908 – 2000

W. V. O. Quine

Naturalized epistemology, behavior, and a deflationary treatment of the mental.

b. 1942

Margaret Gilbert

Joint commitment, plural subjects, and the public structure of shared mind.

b. 1950

Robert Brandom

Inferentialism, normativity, and mindedness as participation in the space of reasons.

1919 – 2001

G. E. M. Anscombe

Intention, action, and an anti-Cartesian grammar of thought and consciousness.

b. 1945

Michael Bratman

Planning agency, self-governance, and the temporally extended structure of intention.

1889 – 1951

Ludwig Wittgenstein

Mind as lived expression, not a private object hidden behind behavior.

1919 – 2006 / b. 1952

P. F. Strawson & Galen Strawson

Persons, subjectivity, and the conceptual grammar of minded agency.

b. 1945

Philip Pettit

Intentional agency, self-regulation, and the norm-governed mind.

1926 – 2018

Stanley Cavell

Acknowledgment, criteria, and the human voice as the scene of mindedness.

1940 – 2022

Raimo Tuomela

Collective intentionality, we-mode agency, and the ontology of social minds.

b. 1978

Christian List

Free will, agency, and non-reductive levels in a naturalistic philosophy of mind.

1912 – 1989

Wilfrid Sellars

Manifest and scientific images, sapience, and the normative form of thought.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
